upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a near-saturated boundary layer at 850mb (4.4°C / 4.4°C dewpoint spread = 0°C), indicating cloud bases close to 1450m MSL and a very moist, stable lower atmosphere. The surface-to-850mb lapse rate is only 6.09°C/km — right at the moist adiabatic rate — meaning thermals are weak and capped near that layer. At 700mb (3000m) winds are 240° at 21 kts, backing to 500mb at 265°/31 kts, indicating increasing westerly flow aloft and potential wind shear above the boundary layer.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for Bay Area. However, METARs show active weather: KSFO reporting FEW008/SCT024 with recent light rain and a wind shift at 1846Z; KAPC reporting BKN036/OVC044 with recent light rain (RAE11) and gusts to 26 kts at 2000Z; KRHV showing BKN028 ceiling with gusts to 22 kts SSW. KHAF reporting 170-180° winds gusting to 27 kts with scattered-to-broken mid-level clouds. Recent precipitation at SFO, Napa, and Half Moon Bay indicates an active frontal passage or trough moving through — ceiling and precip hazards are real across all sites today.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, S/SW-facing, thermal and ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's 1750 ft launch relies on valley thermals and afternoon sea breeze from the SW to S. Ideal conditions are a light-to-moderate S/SW flow 8–18 kts with a developing thermal cycle. Broken cloud cover or overcast suppresses thermal activity significantly.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a NO FLY at Ed Levin 1750 ft. The deciding factor is a completely overcast sky (100% cloud cover all day per both GFS and HRRR), with recent precipitation confirmed at nearby KRHV and KAPC — the frontal trough is actively suppressing any thermal development. Winds at launch are running 15–21 kts from the SSW (190–213°), which is directionally acceptable but the upper end is at the limit, and gusts at KRHV of 22 kts confirm the low-level shear environment. CAPE values of 130–280 J/kg sound promising but are meaningless under a sealed overcast. The BL height oscillates between 470m and 1420m AGL but the saturated sounding (850mb dew point spread = 0°C) means the thermals that do form are weak and cloud-sucking is a real risk. KAPC reported light rain as recently as 2000Z, and precip probability at this site hits 37–82% by late afternoon. Not a day to launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 10–15 kts SSW under 100% overcast; BL only 470–820m AGL. Recent precip in the region. No thermal window possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 15–21 kts SSW with peak at 21 kts at 1 PM — gusty and uneven under solid cloud cover. 80m winds hitting 27 kts at 1 PM indicate rotor/shear potential near launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease slightly to 17–19 kts but precip probability rises sharply to 42–82% by 6–7 PM. Risk of getting caught in rain aloft.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast all day, active precipitation nearby, BL capped by moist stable air, precip risk 37–82% afternoon, wind shear aloft

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill, S-facing, mostly ridge/thermal for newer pilots
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch at Ed Levin is a shorter training hill suitable for soaring when thermals and sea breeze align. Needs S/SW flow, lighter winds preferable (8–15 kts), and clear enough sky for thermal development. Being lower, it is more sheltered but also quicker to shut down in gusty or unstable flow.
sites.todaySetup: NO FLY at the 600 ft launch as well, and arguably more emphatically so for the target P2 audience. The same frontal trough that is wrecking the 1750 ft launch applies here — 100% overcast, recent area precip, and winds running 13–19 kts from the SSW at surface level with gusts. KRHV is showing BKN028 ceilings which at field elevation could put cloud bases uncomfortably close. The 80m wind layer is reading 15–25 kts, indicating mechanical turbulence risk well within the training hill glide path. Precip probability climbs to 81% by 7 PM. This is not a training day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 8–15 kts SSW, 82–100% overcast, BL 560–820m AGL. No thermal development; marginal wind speed but cloud ceiling and area precip are disqualifying.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 12–19 kts SSW, solid overcast, BL bouncing between 755–1320m but sealed by cloud cover. Gusty flow with 80m winds to 25 kts — turbulent for a training hill.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 14–16 kts, precip probability surging to 42–81% by 6–7 PM. Risk of rain during flight increases substantially.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast, nearby active precip, gusty low-level flow, rain risk afternoon/evening, inappropriate for P2 training

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal ridge, W/NW-facing, primarily ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a ridge soaring site that works on W to NW ocean swell-driven sea breeze typically 12–25 kts. It needs wind on the face (W to WNW) without too much northing to avoid rotor below the ridge. Marine layer burn-off timing is critical — if it stays socked in, there's no lift. Onshore flow from the SW or SSW is cross/off-face and reduces soarability.
sites.todaySetup: NO FLY at Mussel Rock today. The wind direction is the primary problem — models show 189–230° (SSW to SW) through most of the day, which is substantially cross to off-face for this W/NW-facing ridge. KHAF confirms the story: 170–180° winds at 15–21 kts gusting to 27 kts, and the SF buoy (46026) is showing 170° at 19.4 kts. That is nearly a direct southerly — no ridge lift possible, and rotor hazard on the lee side of the hill is significant. SST at Half Moon Bay buoy is 14.0°C with a 9-second wave period, suggesting a moderate marine layer that shows up in the KSFO METAR (FEW008 ceiling with recent rain). The KHAF METARs show clouds lifting through the afternoon (BKN070 by 2135Z) but the wind direction never backs to on-face. Rain came through the SFO corridor as recently as 1956Z. Afternoon gusts to 27 kts from the SSW at KHAF would make this a punchy, off-axis mess even for experts.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 6–12 kts SSW, 34–100% cloud cover. Wind direction (189–198°) is off-face; no ridge lift. Marine layer clouds present near coast.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 11–15 kts SSW to S (201–217°), still off-face. Gusts aloft (80m layer: 16–21 kts) add turbulence. Cloud cover 100%.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 15–18 kts SSW, gusts to 29 kts at 80m level. Precip probability rises to 39–85% — rain is likely arriving. Full stop.

verdict.hazards: Wind direction SSW — off-face for W/NW ridge, KHAF gusts to 27 kts, recent coastal rain, marine layer, afternoon precip 39–85%

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal, S/SW-facing, advanced thermal site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works on afternoon valley thermals and the sea breeze funnel from the Fremont gap. Ideal wind is S to SSW at 10–20 kts with a good thermal cycle under partial cloud cover. Like Ed Levin, it needs sun to drive thermals — overcast kills the day.
sites.todaySetup: NO FLY at Mission Peak today. The meteorological picture is essentially identical to Ed Levin — both sites share the same GFS/HRRR grid data and are exposed to the same frontal trough activity. Complete overcast (100% cloud cover all day), with winds 15–21 kts from the SSW (190–213°) at 10m and 19–27 kts at 80m. KAPC had light rain as recently as 2000Z and KRHV has BKN028 ceilings, confirming the suppressed, wet boundary layer. CAPE values of 130–250 J/kg are present but completely useless under cloud cover. Precip probability spikes to 83% by 7 PM, and the afternoon 80m wind layer touching 26–27 kts means wind shear above the peak would make any flight uncomfortable and unpredictable. Save it for another day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 10–15 kts SSW, 94–100% overcast, BL only 470–775m AGL. Stable, wet atmosphere — no thermal activity possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 15–21 kts from SSW, 80m layer 19–27 kts — significant shear at peak height. 100% overcast throughout. Rain possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 17–19 kts SSW, precip probability 14–83% climbing sharply toward evening. Increasingly likely to encounter rain during any flight.

verdict.hazards: 100% overcast all day, active nearby precip, 80m shear winds 19–27 kts, afternoon rain probability 44–83%

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday April 12 looks similarly challenged — 100% overcast all day in both GFS and HRRR, with winds 12–16 kts from the SSW to W (212–261°) and precip probability 29–46%. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 25 kts and even drops visibility to 0.8 km at 1800 with 35% precip chance. The frontal system appears to be lingering or a secondary trough is moving through. Thermal development is zero under this overcast. NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: April 12 at the lower launch mirrors the 1750 ft situation — 100% overcast, 10–14 kts S/SW with gusts to 22 kts, and afternoon precip probability 27–44%. Tomorrow.io confirms cloudy, gusty, and eventually rainy. Not a training day. NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: April 12 could be borderline interesting for wind direction — GFS/HRRR show 250–273° (WSW to W) which is more on-face than today. However, winds are 10–20 kts with 80m layer hitting 27 kts, and Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 35 kts. That's overpowering for most pilots. 100% overcast, CAPE 200–410 J/kg meaninglessly capped. The gust to 35 kts flags this as expert-only conditions at best, and the cloud/precip risk keeps it marginal-to-no-fly. MARGINAL for P4+ locals only in the morning before gusts build.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: April 12 matches Ed Levin — 100% cloud cover, 12–16 kts SSW to W with precip probability 32–43% all day. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 26 kts at 1600 and rain/fog at 1800. No viable thermal window. NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday April 13 shows a marked improvement. GFS shows winds dropping to 2.5–8.9 kts from the NW to WNW (297–316°), cloud cover falling from 18% at 8 AM to 5–37% by midday. BL climbs to 1535–1890m AGL in the afternoon with CAPE 0–30 J/kg — light but possibly soarable thermals. Wind direction from NW is slightly off for classic SSW thermal cycles but the lighter wind and clearing sky make this the best-looking day of the period. Early look: MARGINAL to FLY depending on morning clearing speed.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: April 13 similarly lightens up — winds 2–9 kts from NW/WNW, clouds clearing to 10–85% range. BL height reaches 1535–1890m. Light, cross-ish winds may limit soaring from the lower launch but training conditions could be reasonable by midday. MARGINAL.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: April 13 has light and variable winds 3–13 kts backing from NNW to WNW through the day. Morning cloud cover is 94–100% but clears dramatically by midday (11% by noon). By afternoon (1600Z), winds are 11–14 kts from WNW to W — potentially on-face. This looks like the best day of the stretch for Mussel Rock if the morning clouds clear as modeled. MARGINAL trending FLY by afternoon for P3+.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: April 13 brings a transition — winds 2.5–8.9 kts from WNW/NW, cloud cover variable 18–100% with some clearing windows. BL reaches 1600–1790m by afternoon. Thermals could fire if the sky opens up by midday. MARGINAL; monitor morning clearing.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No data beyond April 13 in the provided dataset. Based on the pattern — frontal clearing by April 13 with light NW winds — April 14 may bring the return of the classic afternoon sea breeze thermal cycle. Watch for a strengthening high building behind the trough.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same outlook as 1750 ft for April 14 — likely improving with the high building. Training conditions could be good if morning clearing is rapid. Monitor.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: If the high continues to build post-April 13, the W to NW sea breeze should firm up favorably for Mussel Rock by April 14. No model data available but the pattern is encouraging.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: April 14 outlook is similar to Ed Levin — likely improving thermal day if the high builds and morning clouds clear. Watch the NW flow direction which could be slightly cross for Mission Peak's optimal SW aspect.

watchlist: Frontal trough/secondary low is the dominant story — active precip confirmed at KSFO, KAPC, KRHV, and KHAF through afternoon today
KHAF gusts to 27 kts from 170-180° — southerly surge affecting the entire coastal corridor including Mussel Rock
KSFO FEW008 ceiling at 2056Z — marine layer and frontal cloud merging; coastal sites socked in through this evening
Ed Levin/Mission Peak 80m winds peaking at 27 kts at 1 PM — significant shear at ridge-top height even if surface winds seem manageable
Tomorrow.io shows 35 kt gusts at Mussel Rock on April 12 — monitor if considering Sunday flight
Monday April 13 is the first potentially flyable day — watch morning cloud burn-off rate and wind direction shift from SSW to NW
tldr: Today is a wash across all four Bay Area sites — a frontal trough has brought active rain, 100% overcast, and off-face southerly winds that disqualify everything from Mussel Rock to Mission Peak. Sunday (April 12) is barely better with persistent overcast and gusty conditions. Your first real shot is Monday April 13 when NW winds lighten to 3–9 kts and the sky finally starts to clear — mark that one on the calendar.
